# DNA Extraction — Story Selling (SS)
**Source:** 19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling / 3.-Story-Selling (7 files)
**Extracted:** 2026-03-10
**Coverage:** Russell Brunson (primary), Michael Hauge (guest, files 4-5), Daegan Smith (guest, files 6-7)
**Total elements:** 68

---

## L1 — PHILOSOPHIES

### PHI-RB-SS-001 — The True Job of Marketing Is to Create Belief
> "If you think about the whole and like, what is the goal and the purpose of marketing? It's to create belief in the minds and the hearts of your customers. And if they honestly believe that what you are saying is true, they're going to follow you. That's it."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Marketing / Persuasion
Selling is a downstream result; belief creation is the upstream cause. Every funnel, webinar, or message exists to install one belief.

---

### PHI-RB-SS-002 — Techno Babble Kills Sales; Epiphany Sells
> "The techno babble is not what got you to buy, it's not what's going to get them to buy... The way you sell stuff is to stop and forget who you are right now, and you've got to come backwards on this journey, and come back here, and remember what was it that gave you this epiphany, because that's the key."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Selling / Communication
Experts accumulate knowledge and then sell FROM that knowledge (logic). Real selling requires going back to the original emotional epiphany and making the prospect relive it.

---

### PHI-RB-SS-003 — Stories Are the Only Mechanism to Give Someone an Epiphany
> "If you guys learn nothing else from this weekend, this is probably the most valuable thing I can give you. When you understand this, this is how you sell. It's not about sweet headlines and like copywriting tricks and secrets, all sorts of stuff. It's just remembering what got you to believe what you do."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Persuasion / Teaching
You cannot force an epiphany — you can only recreate the conditions that led to yours, so the prospect experiences it themselves. Story is the vehicle.

---

### PHI-RB-SS-004 — Emotion Is Created by Conflict, Not by Desire
> "Most of us, a big mistake we make in our stories, is we talk about the desire we have and we're trying to create emotion in our audience by like, oh, I got this new car... But emotion is not created by the desire. Emotion is created by the conflict that's on the path to desire. It's the conflict that makes it interesting."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Storytelling
Beginners describe goals and results. Masters describe obstacles. The gap between desire and attainment is where all emotional engagement lives.

---

### PHI-RB-SS-005 — The Transformation Is What People Care About, Not the Achievement
> "Even though Lightning McQueen never achieved what he was trying to do, the first journey never happened, the second journey of transformation did happen, that's why we love that movie. It's the transformation that we care about, like who did you actually become during this journey, that's what we care about."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Storytelling / Sales
Prospects buy transformation, not information. Stories must show who you BECAME, not just what you ACHIEVED.

---

### PHI-RB-SS-006 — Stories Create Identification, Which Is Deeper Than Trust (via Daegan Smith)
> "We try to engender trust when we market, but if we tell a story, we go past trust and create identification. So the next time you watch a movie, when you're deep into that emotional place where you're like, what's going to happen? You're identifying. You're there."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_3_-_Story_Selling_Blueprint.txt]
**Domínio:** Persuasion / Psychology
Trust = logical. Identification = psychological merger with the character. Identification produces behavior change; trust merely reduces resistance.

---

### PHI-RB-SS-007 — Good Stories Increase Pain of Inaction AND Desire for the Goal Simultaneously
> "Most people know they have a problem as it relates to their goal... the pain of not acting isn't strong enough to push them to really act at that higher level. And if we tell a good story, what happens is we increase that pain of inaction and we also increase the desire for the realization of their goal. So at the end of the day, they must act."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_3_-_Story_Selling_Blueprint.txt]
**Domínio:** Persuasion
A story that only makes someone feel bad (pain) is incomplete. It must simultaneously amplify the vision of success. Both levers pushed together produce action.

---

### PHI-MH-SS-001 — Primary Goal of Story: Elicit Emotion (via Michael Hauge)
> "In other words, there is one thing you must have as your primary goal whenever you're writing a story. And that is you must elicit emotion. People do not watch movies. They don't buy books. They don't go to plays so they can observe the people on the screen laughing, crying, getting turned on, or getting frightened. They go so they can have those experiences themselves."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_4_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_1_.txt]
**Domínio:** Storytelling
Every story decision must serve the goal of eliciting emotion. Plot, structure, and character are all means to that end.

---

### PHI-MH-SS-002 — Identity Is a False Self Created to Avoid Wound Pain (via Michael Hauge)
> "A character's identity is the false self that a character creates to protect herself from the fear that was instilled by the belief that grew out of the wound that happened long, long ago... We have to believe our identities are who we really are. If we're that evolved, we'd already be halfway to our essence."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_5_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_2_.txt]
**Domínio:** Psychology / Character
The most powerful stories show a character dismantling their false self (identity) and stepping into their true self (essence). This is the inner journey.

---

### PHI-MH-SS-003 — Safe vs. Fulfilled: The Fundamental Human Choice (via Michael Hauge)
> "The choice we are all given is this. You can be safe and unfulfilled or you can be fulfilled and scared shitless. And you pay your money and take your choice. As much as they want success and so on, given a choice between safe and happy, we will pick safe most of the time."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_5_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_2_.txt]
**Domínio:** Psychology / Persuasion
This is the core resistance every marketer faces. Stories work by showing the hero choosing fulfilled-and-scared, which gives permission to the audience to do the same.

---

### PHI-DS-SS-001 — The Premise Starts with a Slightly Different Twist on an Old Belief (via Daegan Smith)
> "What we start with is the premise. The premise is an idea that's a breakthrough for ourselves mentally, where we think one thing, but we figure something out, and it's slightly different than what we thought."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_6_-_Heros_Journey_With_Daegan_pt_1.txt]
**Domínio:** Selling / Messaging
A premise is not a claim. It's a paradigm twist — "I always believed X, but discovered Y." The twist must be surprising yet obvious in retrospect.

---

---

## L2 — MENTAL MODELS

### MM-RB-SS-001 — The One Domino / Big Domino Statement
> "If I can make them believe that my new opportunity is the key to whatever they desire most. And it's only attainable through my specific vehicle, then all other objections and concerns become irrelevant and they have to give me money."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Sales Strategy
Before building any funnel or sales message, define the ONE belief that, if installed, makes purchase inevitable. All stories, content, and objection-handling exist to knock down that single domino.

**Formula:**
```
If I can make them believe [NEW OPPORTUNITY] is the key to [DESIRED RESULT]
AND it's only attainable through [SPECIFIC VEHICLE]
THEN all other objections become irrelevant and they must buy.
```

---

### MM-RB-SS-002 — Prolific Zone vs. Red Ocean Positioning
> "It's not true because you are not in the prolific zone. Everyone's teaching how to cut calories. I'm right here in the middle. Not prolific... It's not a new opportunity. I'm not presenting any kind of new opportunity."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Positioning
If your Big Domino can be knocked down without your product (i.e., "just Google it"), you're not in the prolific zone. The vehicle must be unique and non-substitutable.

---

### MM-RB-SS-003 — Emotion → Logic → Fear (The Three Sales Mechanisms)
> "There's three core elements... first one is you're trying to sell people emotionally — people always buy things emotionally. Second, after they've sold themselves emotionally, they have to be sold logically. Third, the way to close is by scarcity — pain of inaction. That's using fear."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_2_-_How_To_Tell_A_Story_The_Correct_Way.txt]
**Domínio:** Sales Psychology
Every complete sales message moves through three phases: emotional identification (story), logical rationalization (case studies/benefits), and urgency/fear (scarcity/pain of inaction).

---

### MM-RB-SS-004 — The Kind-of-Like Bridge (Analogy as Translation Tool)
> "Every time I'm doing, I'm giving a message, and I hit a word that's kind of like techno babble, I have to stop. Because if I don't stop, if I say techno babble word and I keep moving, everyone's like, what did he just say? And they get stuck... Every time I hit a word that's a big confusing word, I have to stop. And I say, you know, it's kind of like, and I link it to something they understand."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Communication / Teaching
The "kind-of-like bridge": whenever encountering jargon, STOP → say "it's kind of like..." → use a concept from their world. Prevents the prospect from mentally checking out.

---

### MM-RB-SS-005 — State Transfer: Getting Them Into the Same State You Were In
> "If I want somebody to have the same epiphany that I had, I can't just tell it to him. I can't just give it to him. What I have to do is I have to get them in the same state that I was in. If I do that, then they'll have the same epiphany that I had."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Persuasion / Storytelling
Emotional state is the container for epiphany. Good storytelling describes sensory/emotional context so thoroughly that the audience inhabits the storyteller's past state — and then reaches the same conclusion organically.

---

### MM-RB-SS-006 — The Hero's Two Journeys Framework Overview
> "Every Hollywood movie from the beginning of time until now, almost every successful movie follows the exact same pattern... There's the Journey of Achievement (outer) — the journey the character is aware of. And then there's the Journey of Transformation (inner). This is the actual journey that we all care about."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Storytelling
Two simultaneous arcs in every compelling story:
- **Outer Journey**: visible goal pursuit (achievement)
- **Inner Journey**: identity → essence transformation (fulfillment)
The outer journey creates plot; the inner journey creates resonance.

---

### MM-RB-SS-007 — Perfect Webinar as 4 Epiphany Bridge Stories
> "If you look at the perfect webinar, outside of the stack and the close, it's basically me telling four epiphany bridge stories. My first story is my origin story... Then belief number one is typically about the vehicle. Number two is about their internal beliefs. Number three is about their external beliefs."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Webinar / Funnel Design
The Perfect Webinar = 4 sequential Epiphany Bridge stories:
1. **Origin Story** → Why this vehicle
2. **Vehicle Story** → Breaking vehicle false belief
3. **Internal Belief Story** → Breaking internal false belief
4. **External Belief Story** → Breaking external false belief

---

### MM-MH-SS-001 — The Six Stages of Story Structure (via Michael Hauge)
> "Any properly structured story is going to consist of six basic stages... defined by five key turning points. Any story you're telling in any medium, it will be strengthened if it hits these five turning points... they occur at exactly the same moment: 10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, and climax."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_4_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_1_.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Structure
Six stages, five turning points at predictable percentages:
1. **Setup** (0-10%) — hero's everyday life
2. **New Situation** (10-25%) — opportunity presented
3. **Progress** (25-50%) — plan seems to be working
4. **Complications & Higher Stakes** (50-75%) — point of no return, stakes rise
5. **Final Push** (75-climax) — after major setback
6. **Aftermath** (post-climax) — new life revealed

---

### MM-MH-SS-002 — The Inner Journey: Wound → Belief → Fear → Identity → Essence (via Michael Hauge)
> "When we are wounded, we acquire or develop a belief... That belief is always going to instill some fear... We have to armor ourselves from what we're afraid of. So what I term an identity is the false self that a character creates to protect herself from the fear... If you strip away all that armor, what's left is the character's essence."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_5_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_2_.txt]
**Domínio:** Psychology / Character Design
The inner journey chain:
```
Wound → Belief (never accurate, always logical) → Fear → Identity (false self)
                                                              ↓ (story journey)
                                                           Essence (true self)
```

---

### MM-MH-SS-003 — Three Ways to Create Empathy With a Character (via Michael Hauge)
> "There are three key ways you do that. Three devices you can use to create that empathy. The first is create sympathy — show them as being the victim of some undeserved misfortune. The second is put the character in jeopardy. The third way is make your hero likeable — show that they're a good person."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_4_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_1_.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Craft
Empathy devices (use 2+ for maximum effect):
1. **Sympathy** — victim of undeserved misfortune
2. **Jeopardy** — risk of losing something vital
3. **Likability** — demonstrably kind, generous, good-hearted person

---

### MM-MH-SS-004 — Five Turning Points Where Customers/Heroes Back Out (via Michael Hauge)
> "Where you put those circles — those are the five places they're most likely to back out. They hear your idea at the opportunity, they don't want it. They back out... They reach the point of no return, that's where they actually have to send the emails or do whatever the next step. That's where they're going to back out."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_5_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_2_.txt]
**Domínio:** Sales / Customer Journey
The five turning points (10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, climax) in a hero's journey are also the five moments customers are most likely to abandon a buying journey. Anticipate resistance at each.

---

### MM-DS-SS-001 — Stories Must Tell One Story, Not Two (via Daegan Smith)
> "I think you are trying to accomplish two separate goals... you're telling two stories. And you need to separate it out and tell one story."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_7_-_Heros_Journey_With_Daegan_pt_2.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Craft
The most common story-selling failure: cramming two different epiphanies/journeys into one narrative. Each sales story must have exactly ONE premise, ONE outer motivation, ONE transformation.

---

---

## L3 — HEURISTICS

### HEUR-RB-SS-001 — Sell Only One Belief (Never Multiple)
> "Perry Belcher said: we went through all the offers we've created in the last 10 years. Anytime we ask somebody to believe more than one thing in our sales message, it was always a bomb. If the message, the core message got them to believe just one thing, those were our winners every single time."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Sales / Copywriting
**Rule:** One belief = winner. Two or more beliefs = failure. All sales messages must identify the single belief that, if installed, makes purchase inevitable.

---

### HEUR-RB-SS-002 — Ask "Why" 7-8 Times to Reach Internal Desire
> "To get to the internal desire, a lot of times you'll say, here's the external desire... and to get the internal, you usually ask why, about seven or eight times... and eventually you'll get to the real internal desires, which typically are related to: you want to feel loved, you want to feel acceptance, you want to change the world."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Copywriting / Research
**Rule:** Ask "why?" 7-8 levels deep to get past surface desires to core emotional drivers (love, acceptance, respect, legacy). Surface desires don't sell; core desires do.

---

### HEUR-RB-SS-003 — First 10% of Story Builds Relationship With Hero
> "Now, Michael Hay, so there's five ways that in Hollywood they try to build relationships with the character. And a good character usually has two of these five things... the first 10% of every single movie, the goal is to build a relationship with the hero."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Structure
**Rule:** The first 10% of any story is non-negotiable relationship-building time. Launching into the plot before the audience cares about the hero kills emotional engagement.

---

### HEUR-RB-SS-004 — 5 Ways to Build Relationship With Character (need 2 of 5)
> "So first thing the character needs to be, again, two of these five things. So they can be a victim, okay? They could be in jeopardy, they need to be really, really likable, they can be funny, or they can be powerful."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Craft
**Rule:** Ensure the hero of your story exhibits at least 2 of: victim, in jeopardy, likable, funny, powerful. Characters with only one trait feel flat; characters with 2+ earn audience investment.

---

### HEUR-RB-SS-005 — The Epiphany Bridge Story Has 8 Required Questions
> "I broke this down into some questions... there's eight questions, the backstory, your desires, the wall, the epiphany, the plan, the conflict, achievement, and transformation."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Selling
**8 required questions for an Epiphany Bridge Script:**
1. What is your backstory? (create rapport)
2. What were your desires? (external + internal)
3. What was the wall? (current opportunity failing)
4. What was the epiphany? (discovery of new opportunity)
5. What was the plan you created?
6. What was the conflict you hit?
7. What was the achievement?
8. What was the transformation?

---

### HEUR-RB-SS-006 — Story Selling Produces 3-Lever Sales: Emotion + Logic + Scarcity
> "Addresses logic... the case studies — this is where you're logically selling them on that concept... And by the way the third — the third way to close on a bike here — by scarcity... addresses pain of inaction... three things: emotion, logic, and fear."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_2_-_How_To_Tell_A_Story_The_Correct_Way.txt]
**Domínio:** Sales
**Rule:** A complete story-selling sequence always deploys all three: emotion (story/backstory/epiphany), logic (case studies/benefits/objection transformation), fear (scarcity/pain of inaction).

---

### HEUR-RB-SS-007 — Hidden Benefits Tip the Decision
> "Instead of saying the great thing about this idea is x, y, and z, it's a lot better to say we all know that idea x is going to give us a, b, c, and d. But what people don't see is e, f, and g. And those are really the most awesome things... the person goes, holy shit, it's way better than I thought."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_3_-_Story_Selling_Blueprint.txt]
**Domínio:** Copywriting
**Rule:** After stating the obvious benefits, reveal 2-3 "hidden benefits" they didn't expect. This creates a surprise-value moment that overwhelms remaining price objections.

---

### HEUR-RB-SS-008 — Case Studies Detach the Idea From the Storyteller
> "What case studies do is we detach this idea as an epiphany from us. So if I have a great idea, up until this point people will say, well that's awesome for you, but that's not going to happen for me. But right here — this works for everybody. This guy, and this guy... Now they start to own the idea."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_3_-_Story_Selling_Blueprint.txt]
**Domínio:** Sales / Copywriting
**Rule:** Insert case studies AFTER the epiphany, not before. Their function is to transfer ownership of the idea from "Russell's idea" to "my idea." Without them, the prospect says "works for him, not me."

---

### HEUR-DS-SS-001 — Story Open at Point of Highest Drama, Not Chronological Start (via Daegan Smith)
> "Good movies start at the peak — the highest point of drama possible... If you look at like really good movies, movies don't start really really boring and work to the exciting part five minutes in."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_2_-_How_To_Tell_A_Story_The_Correct_Way.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Craft
**Rule:** Open stories at maximum drama, then jump back to backstory. Never build chronologically toward excitement — start AT the crisis.

---

### HEUR-DS-SS-002 — Start with High Drama → Jump Back to Backstory
> "You always want to start a story at the point of high drama... and this is such a key thing. Start the point of high drama, then come back to backstory."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_2_-_How_To_Tell_A_Story_The_Correct_Way.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Structure
**Sequence:** (1) Open at dramatic crisis → (2) Go back to backstory → (3) Travel back through to epiphany → (4) Continue forward to transformation.

---

### HEUR-DS-SS-003 — The Insurmountable Object Creates Epiphany (via Daegan Smith)
> "In storytelling terms, it's known as the insurmountable object. It's that point of ultimate peril where you feel like how the hell is the character going to get out of this?... If we use this moment to create an epiphany, here's a simple little thing that I did, and all this other great stuff happened."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_3_-_Story_Selling_Blueprint.txt]
**Domínio:** Storytelling
**Rule:** The epiphany must emerge FROM the moment of greatest peril. The darker the pit, the more powerful the "I found the answer" moment. Never place the epiphany in a comfortable scene.

---

### HEUR-MH-SS-001 — Outer Motivation Must Be Visible and Specific (via Michael Hauge)
> "An outer motivation is one that when you hear what it is, you immediately get a picture of what achieving that goal would look like on the screen. And equally important, your vision will be the same as everybody else's... 'high school girl who wants to win the statewide cheerleading championship' — now all of you just got a picture."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_4_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_1_.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Craft
**Test:** Can everyone picture the same scene when you describe the hero's goal? If visions diverge, the goal is too abstract. Concrete, visual goals create shared emotional investment.

---

### HEUR-MH-SS-002 — Wound-Derived Beliefs Are Never Accurate But Always Logical (via Michael Hauge)
> "Beliefs that grow out of wounds are never accurate, but they are always logical. Take Rain Man. Here's a guy when he was like two and a half or three, his brother was taken away, his mother died and his father abandoned him. What's his belief? Anybody I love disappears. Well he only had three people in his whole life and they all disappeared and they were the ones he loved. It's a very logical belief. Isn't true, it is not accurate, but it always makes sense."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_5_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_2_.txt]
**Domínio:** Psychology / Empathy
**Rule:** To generate empathy for your hero's limiting beliefs, show the wound that created them. The audience will understand (even if not agree) because wounds always justify beliefs logically.

---

### HEUR-MH-SS-003 — If Hero Wins, They Must Have Moved from Identity to Essence (via Michael Hauge)
> "If the hero of a Hollywood movie wins, they must have left their identity and moved into their essence. If they do not leave their identity and move into their essence, they must lose. That's a tragedy."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_5_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_2_.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Craft
**Rule:** In any success story (yours or a client's), the outer achievement must be accompanied by an inner transformation. If the person achieves the result but doesn't change who they are, the audience feels hollow.

---

### HEUR-MH-SS-004 — 5 Backstory Elements Must Appear in First 10% (via Michael Hauge)
> "Several things you must do at the beginning of your story: you must introduce the hero, you must show that hero living her everyday life, and you must create empathy with the character."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_4_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_1_.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Structure
**First 10% checklist:**
- [ ] Introduce the hero
- [ ] Show everyday life (before picture)
- [ ] Create empathy (sympathy + jeopardy + likability)
- [ ] Hint at the wound/identity
- [ ] Establish the world they're about to leave

---

### HEUR-DS-SS-004 — The Invisible Funnel: 84.65% Attendance Rate with Story-Framed Free Events (via Daegan Smith)
> "193 attended, and they stayed the entire time. Literally it's 84.65% attended... with this reframing of it from just come and let me teach you some stuff, it has a tangible expense. You're giving me your credit card information, and you get to judge me. Creates this just focus that they want to call you out."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_6_-_Heros_Journey_With_Daegan_pt_1.txt]
**Domínio:** Marketing / Conversion
**Benchmark:** Invisible Funnel structure (story-framed free event + pay only if you like it) produces ~85% webinar attendance vs. typical 30-40%. 50% conversion to applications; 27 clients from 193 attendees at $10-15K each.

---

---

## L4 — FRAMEWORKS

### FW-RB-SS-001 — The Epiphany Bridge Script (8-Part Story Structure)
> "We call this, inside the inner circle, the Epiphany Bridge Script... it starts over here with the backstory... after you introduce the backstory and talk about the desire, there's two things: external desires and internal desires... you hit this wall... you have this epiphany... the plan... the conflict... achievement... transformation."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Selling

```
EPIPHANY BRIDGE SCRIPT
═══════════════════════
1. BACKSTORY     → Build rapport; they care if you succeed
2. DESIRES       → External (what) + Internal (why × 7-8)
3. THE WALL      → Current opportunity that's failing them
4. THE EPIPHANY  → Discovery of the new opportunity/vehicle
5. THE PLAN      → How you intended to use the new opportunity
6. THE CONFLICT  → What almost stopped you
7. ACHIEVEMENT   → What you got (results)
8. TRANSFORMATION→ Who you became (fulfillment)
```
Used in: Origin Story, Vehicle Story, Internal Belief Story, External Belief Story.

---

### FW-RB-SS-002 — The Hero's Two Journeys (Outer + Inner) Applied to Marketing
> "There are two journeys happening simultaneously in every story that works. The Journey of Achievement — what the hero is going after. And the Journey of Transformation — who they become. You need both."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Storytelling / Marketing

```
OUTER JOURNEY (Journey of Achievement)
─────────────────────────────────────
Visible goal → Obstacles → Conflict → Achievement/Failure
Purpose: Creates plot and forward momentum

INNER JOURNEY (Journey of Transformation)
──────────────────────────────────────────
Identity (false self) → Wound exposed → Fear confronted → Essence (true self)
Purpose: Creates emotional resonance and lasting connection
```
In marketing: outer = product result; inner = identity shift the buyer experiences.

---

### FW-RB-SS-003 — The Story Selling Process Map (Daegan Smith's Full Arc)
> "This graph right here is kind of the end of a lot of work, a lot of testing. It starts at top left and it goes to the right and it curves down and comes back and ends down here at the invitation."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_2_-_How_To_Tell_A_Story_The_Correct_Way.txt]
**Domínio:** Sales / Funnel Copy (via Daegan Smith)

```
STORY SELLING PROCESS MAP
══════════════════════════
1. POINT OF HIGH DRAMA      → Open at crisis (not chronological start)
2. BACKSTORY                → Where you were; build empathy
3. THE WALL                 → What stopped progress
4. EPIPHANY                 → The breakthrough idea
5. IDEA EXPLOSION           → All possibilities that opened up
6. YES/NO IMPASSE           → The choice; acknowledge fear
7. FIRST SIGNS OF SUCCESS   → Small early win after choosing yes
8. RAPID GROWTH             → Things getting better
9. CASE STUDIES             → Logic sell; idea works for everyone
10. HIDDEN BENEFITS         → Surprise: it's even better than expected
11. OBJECTION TRANSFORMATION→ Convert objections into reasons to buy
12. THE ULTIMATE TWIST      → Pre-sell the product reveal
13. MISTAKES/INOCULATION    → Don't make these errors (builds authority)
14. ULTIMATE SOLUTION       → Reveal the product/offer
15. INVITATION/CLOSE        → Scarcity + pain of inaction
```

---

### FW-RB-SS-004 — The Big Domino Statement Formula
> "This is the big domino statement: If I can make them believe that my new opportunity is the key to whatever they desire most, and it's only attainable through my specific vehicle, then all other objections and concerns become irrelevant and they have to give me money."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Positioning / Sales Strategy

**Validation test for Big Domino Statement:**
- [ ] Is it in the Prolific Zone? (not commoditized)
- [ ] Is it a New Opportunity? (not an improvement offer)
- [ ] Is it only attainable through your vehicle? (not substitutable)
- [ ] If they believe it, MUST they buy? (no logical escape route)

---

### FW-MH-SS-001 — Six-Stage Story Structure with Five Turning Points (via Michael Hauge)
> "Any properly structured story is going to consist of six basic stages... defined by five key turning points. The setup, new situation, progress, complications & higher stakes, final push, aftermath."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_4_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_1_.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Structure

```
SIX STAGES WITH PERCENTAGE MARKS
══════════════════════════════════
Stage 1: SETUP (0-10%)
  → Introduce hero, everyday life, create empathy
  Turning Point 1 [10%]: OPPORTUNITY — something new happens, creates desire

Stage 2: NEW SITUATION (10-25%)
  → Hero pursuing preliminary goal, figuring it out
  Turning Point 2 [25%]: CHANGE OF PLANS — bigger desire locked in, visible outer motivation begins

Stage 3: PROGRESS (25-50%)
  → Plan seems to be working, momentum builds
  Turning Point 3 [50%]: POINT OF NO RETURN — hero fully commits, burns bridges

Stage 4: COMPLICATIONS & HIGHER STAKES (50-75%)
  → Obstacles multiply, stakes raise dramatically
  Turning Point 4 [75%]: MAJOR SETBACK — all seems lost, hero tries to retreat

Stage 5: FINAL PUSH (75-climax)
  → Reflection character snaps hero out of retreat, last attempt

  Turning Point 5: CLIMAX — win or lose (must be resolved)

Stage 6: AFTERMATH (post-climax)
  → Before/after contrast; new life revealed
```

---

### FW-MH-SS-002 — The Inner Journey Chain (Identity to Essence) (via Michael Hauge)
> "The inner journey for your hero is a journey from living in his identity to living in his essence. And that's what's known in storytelling circles as a character's arc."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_5_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_2_.txt]
**Domínio:** Character / Story

```
INNER JOURNEY CHAIN
═══════════════════
WOUND (painful past experience)
  ↓
BELIEF (logical but inaccurate — "if I try, people will hurt me")
  ↓
FEAR (of re-experiencing the wound)
  ↓
IDENTITY (false self created as armor — e.g., "I'm the scary ogre")
  ↓ (story journey forces confrontation)
LONGING / NEED (what hero secretly wants but won't pursue)
  ↓
ESSENCE (true self — who they'd be if they had courage)

Inner Journey = courage to dismantle identity and live in essence
If hero wins outer journey, inner journey MUST complete.
```

---

### FW-MH-SS-003 — Five Character Empathy Devices (Hierarchy) (via Michael Hauge)
> "There are five ways that in Hollywood they try to build relationships with the character. And a good character usually has two of these five things."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Craft

```
FIVE EMPATHY DEVICES (use 2+)
══════════════════════════════
1. VICTIM     — undeserved misfortune; we feel sorry
2. JEOPARDY   — risk of losing something vital; we worry
3. LIKABLE    — kind, generous, good-hearted; we root for
4. FUNNY      — makes us laugh; we enjoy being with them
5. POWERFUL   — competent, impressive; we're drawn to them

Setup: Must establish 2+ in first 10% of story.
Marketing application: Use in backstory section of Epiphany Bridge.
```

---

### FW-DS-SS-001 — The Invisible Funnel Model (via Daegan Smith)
> "The proposition is, let me teach you all this stuff. Don't pay me a dime. Let me teach you all this stuff. If it's worth it, at the end, I happily, humbly accept the ticket fee of $97. If not, I'll give you an email address. Just send me an email. You don't have to pay a dime."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_6_-_Heros_Journey_With_Daegan_pt_1.txt]
**Domínio:** Funnel Design

```
INVISIBLE FUNNEL STRUCTURE (Daegan Smith)
══════════════════════════════════════════
1. HOOK EMAIL       → Story-based hook (e.g., "from bankruptcy to $286K/month")
2. FREE REGISTRATION→ Standard webinar registration page
3. FREE WEBINAR 1   → Story-selling event; install epiphany; over-deliver
                      • Engage audience live (response rate target: 80%+)
                      • Tell complete story with premise and insight
4. FREE WEBINAR 2   → Reveal full system; application process introduced
5. APPLICATION      → Written form; creates investment and filtering
6. PHONE CLOSE      → Ops manager calls qualified applicants; close ~60%
7. HIGH-TICKET OFFER→ $10-15K+ for "done with you" or "done for you"

Metrics from event with 193 attendees:
- 84.65% attendance rate
- 97 applications (50% of attendees)
- 27 clients chosen (~28% of applicants)
- ~$315,000+ revenue
```

---

---

## L5 — METHODOLOGIES

### MET-RB-SS-001 — How to Create the Big Domino Statement (Step-by-Step)
> "For all of you guys, it's going to be different. And what's the next thing? I'm going to show you guys a couple of examples... if what you have doesn't fit, if it's not a true statement, then these are the reasons why."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Positioning / Sales Strategy

```
CREATING YOUR BIG DOMINO STATEMENT
════════════════════════════════════
Step 1: Write draft using template:
  "If I can make them believe [VEHICLE] is the key to [DESIRED RESULT]
   and it's only attainable through [MY SPECIFIC OFFER]..."

Step 2: Validate against 4 tests:
  □ PROLIFIC ZONE: Is it unique, or is everyone teaching this?
  □ NEW OPPORTUNITY: Is it a new vehicle, not an "improvement"?
  □ ONLY ATTAINABLE: Can they get the same result without me?
  □ MUST BUY: If they believed this 100%, would they have to buy?

Step 3: If any test fails, diagnose:
  • Fails prolific: You're in a red ocean → create a new mechanism
  • Fails new opportunity: You're selling improvement → reframe as vehicle
  • Fails only attainable: Find what's unique to your method
  • Fails must buy: Tighten the connection between vehicle and result

Step 4: Use this as your North Star for all story content.
        Every epiphany bridge story should knock down this one domino.
```

---

### MET-RB-SS-002 — How to Tell the Epiphany Bridge Story (Performance Method)
> "So I'm going to go through and tell you guys epiphany bridge story, the best I can. All right, so it starts with a backstory. This is 15 years [married]..."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_1_-_Story_Selling-005.txt]
**Domínio:** Storytelling / Presenting

```
TELLING THE EPIPHANY BRIDGE STORY
═══════════════════════════════════
Phase 1 — BACKSTORY (Build Rapport)
  • Start in the "before" — who you were, what you wanted
  • Share BOTH external desire (visible goal) AND internal desire (core need)
  • Internal desire requires 7-8 "whys" — go deep
  • Build character: use 2+ of (victim/jeopardy/likable/funny/powerful)

Phase 2 — THE WALL (Create Empathy)
  • Describe the current opportunity that stopped working
  • This is where your audience IS RIGHT NOW
  • Don't rush through it — they need to feel seen

Phase 3 — THE EPIPHANY (State Transfer)
  • Describe the exact moment/context of discovery
  • Re-create your emotional state at that moment (not just what happened)
  • Use: sensory details, internal monologue, physical sensations
  • The audience must FEEL what you felt, not just hear what happened

Phase 4 — THE PLAN (Build Desire)
  • Share the "idea explosion" — all the possibilities you saw
  • Let them imagine their own possibilities

Phase 5 — THE CONFLICT (Maintain Tension)
  • What almost stopped you? What went wrong?
  • Conflict = emotional engagement; skip this = flat story

Phase 6 — ACHIEVEMENT + TRANSFORMATION (Close)
  • Achievement: the outer result (numbers, wins)
  • Transformation: who you became (the more important part)
  • Transformation = "that's what I want" trigger in audience

Avoid: Techno babble. Use "kind-of-like" bridges for every unfamiliar term.
```

---

### MET-MH-SS-001 — How to Develop a Hero's Inner Journey for Your Story (via Michael Hauge)
> "To use and employ the inner journey in the stories you're creating, you've got to start by asking these questions about the hero of your story."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_5_-_The_Heros_2_Journeys__Michael_Hauge_pt_2_.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Craft / Character Development

```
DEVELOPING THE HERO'S INNER JOURNEY
══════════════════════════════════════
Step 1 — LONGING / NEED
  Ask: What does the hero deeply desire but is too afraid to pursue?
  (Longing = admits it but won't pursue; Need = won't even admit it)

Step 2 — WOUND
  Ask: What painful past experience is still running this character?
  Usually from adolescence. Must be emotionally real and specific.

Step 3 — BELIEF
  Ask: What belief did this wound create?
  Test: Is it logical? (it must be) Is it accurate? (it must NOT be)
  Format: "Because of [wound], I believe that [false rule about world]"

Step 4 — FEAR
  Ask: What is the hero afraid of, as a result of this belief?
  Format: "If I [take the risk], I will [re-experience wound pain]"

Step 5 — IDENTITY
  Ask: What false self has the hero constructed to avoid that fear?
  This is the mask, the armor, the persona they present to world.

Step 6 — ESSENCE
  Ask: Who would this person be without that armor?
  This is who they become at the end of the story.

Step 7 — INTEGRATE INTO OUTER JOURNEY
  Map inner journey stages to outer journey stages (same 6 stages)
  At setup: hero fully in identity
  At climax: hero must step into essence to win
  At aftermath: show them living in essence
```

---

### MET-DS-SS-001 — How to Reconstruct a Story From Scratch (Story Coaching Method) (via Daegan Smith / Michael Hauge)
> "What I really want to do is we're going to rebuild this story from the ground up. Take all of the things about this story, put them in a basket and set it off in the corner. Nothing is sacrosanct."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_7_-_Heros_Journey_With_Daegan_pt_2.txt]
**Domínio:** Story Development

```
STORY RECONSTRUCTION PROCESS (Michael Hauge Coaching Method)
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
Step 1 — CLARIFY PURPOSE
  □ Is this story to persuade (belief/action)?
  □ Is this story to create emotional experience?
  □ Is this story to teach?
  (Most failing stories try to do all three simultaneously)

Step 2 — IDENTIFY ONE OUTER MOTIVATION
  □ What is the single, visible goal the hero is pursuing?
  □ Can you describe it in one sentence that creates the same picture for everyone?
  □ When would the hero say "I did it"?

Step 3 — IDENTIFY ONE STORY (not two)
  □ Is there more than one premise? Split into separate stories.
  □ Does the story change purpose midway? Identify the split point.

Step 4 — FIND THE INNER JOURNEY (Wound → Essence)
  □ What courage did the hero have at the END they didn't have at the BEGINNING?
  □ What identity was the hero living in at the start?
  □ What essence did they step into at the climax?

Step 5 — BUILD STRUCTURE (Six Stages)
  □ Where does the setup end? (10%)
  □ Where does the outer motivation begin? (25%)
  □ Where is the point of no return? (50%)
  □ Where is the major setback? (75%)
  □ Where is the climax?
  □ What is the aftermath?

Step 6 — REWRITE FROM SCRATCH
  Do not "move chess pieces." Rebuild with clear structure.
  Old material goes in the basket — retrieve what fits the new structure.
```

---

### MET-DS-SS-002 — Story-Selling Email Sequence Structure (Soap Opera Format) (via Daegan Smith)
> "I also have a email series that I go through this exact same path and bring up over seven or eight different days... see the part of the story on each day... I drag it out so that they get parts of each day but it keeps them engaged, keeps them wanting to watch the webinar, keeps them wanting to come back for more."
^[RAIZ:/knowledge/external/sources/russell-brunson/raw/19._24hr_Expert_Story_Selling/3.-Story-Selling/transcription_2_-_How_To_Tell_A_Story_The_Correct_Way.txt]
**Domínio:** Email Marketing / Funnel

```
STORY-SELLING SOAP OPERA EMAIL SEQUENCE
════════════════════════════════════════
Structure: 7-8 emails, each revealing one story segment

Email 1: POINT OF HIGH DRAMA
  Subject: "The day my [X] failed me"
  Content: Open at crisis, don't resolve it

Email 2: BACKSTORY
  Subject: "How it all started..."
  Content: Who you were before; seeds of the coming crisis

Email 3: THE WALL + EPIPHANY SETUP
  Subject: "The thing nobody told me about [niche]"
  Content: What wasn't working; moment of total failure

Email 4: THE EPIPHANY (withhold what it is)
  Subject: "6 words that changed everything"
  Content: Tell the emotional state, tease the discovery

Email 5: THE IDEA EXPLOSION
  Subject: "What I realized when it clicked"
  Content: All the possibilities that opened up; invite imagination

Email 6: ACHIEVEMENT + CASE STUDY
  Subject: "Here's what happened next"
  Content: Results; introduce other success stories

Email 7-8: INVITATION
  Subject: "[Tomorrow/Last chance] — the full system"
  Content: Reveal the product; pain of inaction + scarcity

Key principle: Each email ends at a cliffhanger.
               Curiosity gap drives click-through to webinar.
```
